beginner.question: How do you set the font family in CSS?
beginner.answer: Use the font-family property: font-family: 'Open Sans', Arial, sans-serif;. You specify a prioritized list of fonts (a font stack), separated by commas. The browser uses the first available font. Always end the stack with a generic family keyword (serif, sans-serif, monospace, cursive, fantasy) as a fallback. Multi-word font names must be quoted.

beginner.question: What are the five generic font families in CSS?
beginner.answer: The five generic families are: serif (fonts with decorative strokes at character ends, like Times New Roman), sans-serif (fonts without serifs, like Arial), monospace (fixed-width fonts, like Courier New), cursive (handwriting-style fonts, like Comic Sans), and fantasy (decorative/ornamental fonts). Always include a generic family as the final fallback in any font stack.

beginner.question: How do you set font size in CSS and what units can you use?
beginner.answer: Use font-size with various units: px (absolute pixels), em (relative to parent's font-size), rem (relative to root/html font-size), % (percentage of parent), vw (viewport width), or keywords (small, medium, large). Best practice is to use rem for body text to respect user browser font-size settings, and em for component-relative sizing.

beginner.question: What is the difference between font-weight: bold and font-weight: 700?
beginner.answer: There is no difference — font-weight: bold is equivalent to font-weight: 700. The numeric scale runs 100-900: 100 (Thin), 200 (Extra Light), 300 (Light), 400 (Normal/Regular), 500 (Medium), 600 (Semi Bold), 700 (Bold), 800 (Extra Bold), 900 (Black). Not all fonts have all weights available — if a specific weight doesn't exist, the browser approximates it or uses the nearest available weight.

beginner.question: How do you make text italic in CSS?
beginner.answer: Use font-style: italic;. This selects the italic variant of the font (a specially designed cursive version). font-style: oblique; is similar but slants the regular (roman) font without using an italic design. Most fonts have italic but not oblique. font-style: normal; removes italic. The difference between italic and oblique is typographic: italic is a distinct typeface design, while oblique is just the regular face mechanically slanted.

beginner.question: What is the font shorthand and what order do the values go in?
beginner.answer: The font shorthand syntax: font: style weight size/line-height family;. Example: font: italic bold 16px/1.5 'Arial', sans-serif;. Only font-size and font-family are required. The order is strict: style, variant, weight come first (in any order), then size (required), optionally /line-height, then family (required). Missing values that are not required reset to their defaults.

beginner.question: How do you use the @font-face rule to load custom fonts?
beginner.answer: @font-face defines a custom font: @font-face { font-family: 'MyFont'; src: url('myfont.woff2') format('woff2'); font-weight: normal; font-style: normal; font-display: swap; }. Then use it: body { font-family: 'MyFont', sans-serif; }. Provide multiple src formats for browser compatibility. WOFF2 is the most modern and efficient format.

beginner.question: What is a font stack and why is it important?
beginner.answer: A font stack is a prioritized list of font families in the font-family property. The browser tries each font in order and uses the first one installed on the user's system. Example: font-family: 'Helvetica Neue', Helvetica, Arial, sans-serif;. Font stacks are important because no single font is available on all systems — the stack ensures every user sees a font in the desired style, falling back to similar fonts.

beginner.question: What is line-height and how is it related to fonts?
beginner.answer: line-height controls the vertical space between lines of text. It's often specified in the font shorthand after font-size with a slash: font: 16px/1.5 Arial; means font-size 16px and line-height 1.5 (24px total). A unitless line-height (like 1.5) is recommended because it's relative to the font-size and is properly inherited by child elements. line-height affects readability significantly.

beginner.question: What is the default font-size in most browsers?
beginner.answer: The default font-size in most browsers is 16px for the root element (<html>). This can be changed by the user in browser settings, which is why using relative units (rem, em) is important for accessibility. Many developers set html { font-size: 62.5%; } to make 1rem = 10px for easier calculations while still respecting user preferences.

intermediate.question: What is the difference between em and rem units for font-size?
intermediate.answer: em is relative to the parent element's font-size. If a parent has font-size: 20px, a child with font-size: 0.5em gets 10px. This compounds — nested elements with em can grow/shrink unpredictably. rem (root em) is always relative to the root element's font-size (<html>), typically 16px. rem doesn't compound, making it more predictable. rem is recommended for most sizing to respect user preferences while avoiding compounding issues.

intermediate.question: How do you use Google Fonts in a project?
intermediate.answer: Include the Google Fonts stylesheet link in your HTML <head>: <link href="https://fonts.googleapis.com/css2?family=Roboto:wght@400;700&display=swap" rel="stylesheet">. Then reference it in CSS: body { font-family: 'Roboto', sans-serif; }. You specify which weights and styles you need in the URL to reduce file size. The display=swap parameter ensures text remains visible while the font loads.

intermediate.question: What does font-display: swap do and why is it important?
intermediate.answer: font-display: swap tells the browser to show text immediately using a fallback (system) font while the custom web font is downloading, then swap to the custom font once loaded. This prevents Flash of Invisible Text (FOIT) where text is invisible during font loading. The trade-off is a Flash of Unstyled Text (FOUT) where the text briefly appears in the fallback font before switching. font-display: swap is recommended for most web fonts to prioritize content readability.

intermediate.question: What are the different font-display values and when should each be used?
intermediate.answer: auto — browser default (usually block period of ~3 seconds). block — short block period (~3s of invisible text), then swap. swap — text shows in fallback immediately, swaps when font loads (recommended for most content). fallback — short block (~100ms), then fallback for ~3s, then keep fallback if font hasn't loaded. optional — very short block (~100ms), then fallback; browser may skip download entirely on slow connections (good for performance).

intermediate.question: How does variable fonts technology work in CSS?
intermediate.answer: Variable fonts combine multiple font variations (weight, width, slant, etc.) into a single file using axes of variation. Use font-variation-settings to control axes: font-variation-settings: 'wght' 700, 'wdth' 75;. Standard axes include: wght (weight), wdth (width), ital (italic), slnt (slant), opsz (optical size). Variable fonts reduce file size (one file instead of multiple weight files) and enable smooth transitions between weights.

intermediate.question: What is the system font stack and why would you use it?
intermediate.answer: The system font stack uses the native font of each operating system: font-family: -apple-system, BlinkMacSystemFont, 'Segoe UI', Roboto, Oxygen, Ubuntu, Cantarell, sans-serif;. Benefits: (1) Zero download time (no web fonts needed); (2) Matches the OS look and feels native; (3) Best performance; (4) No FOIT/FOUT. System fonts are ideal for UI text (navigation, buttons, labels) where brand font is less critical.

intermediate.question: How do you handle font rendering differences across operating systems?
intermediate.answer: Font rendering differs significantly: macOS uses Core Text (smoother, more weight), Windows uses DirectWrite/ClearType (crisper, lighter), Linux uses FreeType (varies). Strategies include: (1) Use a well-tested web font that renders consistently; (2) Use font-smoothing: -webkit-font-smoothing: antialiased; for macOS (though this is a non-standard property and not recommended); (3) Use different font stacks per OS with media queries; (4) Test on all target platforms.

intermediate.question: What is the font-size-adjust property and when is it useful?
intermediate.answer: font-size-adjust adjusts the font size to ensure consistent x-height (the height of lowercase letters like 'x') across different fallback fonts. If your primary font has a small x-height but the fallback has a large x-height, the text appears larger when the font swaps. font-size-adjust: 0.5; forces the fallback font's x-height to match the primary font's x-height. This is important for maintaining layout stability during FOUT with font-display: swap.

intermediate.question: How do you use font-variant: small-caps?
intermediate.answer: font-variant: small-caps renders lowercase letters as smaller uppercase letters. It creates a petite caps effect where the uppercase letters remain full-size and lowercase letters become smaller uppercase versions. This is different from text-transform: uppercase, which makes all letters full uppercase. Small caps are often used for abbreviations, acronyms, headers, and stylistic text effects.

intermediate.question: What is the difference between font-style: italic and font-style: oblique?
intermediate.answer: Both slant text, but italic uses a specially designed italic typeface (different letterforms, more cursive), while oblique is the regular (roman) typeface mechanically slanted. Most web fonts include italic (regular + italic files) but rarely include oblique. On fonts that lack an italic variant, the browser simulates it by slanting the regular font (synthetic italic), which is essentially oblique.

advanced.question: Explain the complete font loading lifecycle and how browsers handle web font loading events.
advanced.answer: The font loading lifecycle: (1) CSS parser encounters @font-face or font-family referencing a web font; (2) Browser checks cache — if present, uses immediately; (3) If not cached, starts download; (4) During download, font-display determines behavior (block/swap/fallback/optional); (5) Font download completes and is decoded; (6) If font-display: swap, the text is re-rendered with the web font, causing layout shift if metrics differ; (7) The CSS Font Loading API (document.fonts) emits events (loading, loadingerror, loaded) for JavaScript control.

advanced.question: How do you subset fonts to reduce file size and what are the trade-offs?
advanced.answer: Font subsetting removes unused characters (glyphs) from the font file, drastically reducing file size. For example, a Latin-only subset might reduce a font from 200KB to 20KB. Tools like glyphhanger, fonttools, or Google Fonts' &text= parameter can create subsets. Trade-offs: (1) Missing glyphs fail silently or show the .notdef character (a blank box/tofu); (2) Dynamic content (user-generated text) might use characters not in the subset; (3) Unicode-range subsetting with @font-face's unicode-range property can load different subsets for different character ranges.

advanced.question: Explain the FOUT (Flash of Unstyled Text) and FOIT (Flash of Invisible Text) strategies and their impact on Cumulative Layout Shift (CLS).
advanced.answer: FOIT (font-display: block/auto) hides text for up to 3 seconds while the font loads, then swaps. This creates invisible text (bad for readability) but no layout shift if the fallback metrics happen to match. FOUT (font-display: swap) shows fallback text immediately, then swaps to the web font — content is readable immediately but CLS occurs when the web font has different metrics. FOUT is preferred for content readability, but font-size-adjust and careful fallback font selection can minimize CLS. The ideal is finding fallback fonts with similar metrics to the web font.

advanced.question: How does the unicode-range descriptor in @font-face work for loading specific character subsets?
advanced.answer: unicode-range specifies which Unicode characters a @font-face rule applies to. Example: @font-face { font-family: 'MyFont'; src: url('latin.woff2'); unicode-range: U+0000-00FF; } @font-face { font-family: 'MyFont'; src: url('cjk.woff2'); unicode-range: U+4E00-9FFF; }. The browser selects the appropriate font file based on the text's character range. This allows loading separate files for Latin, Cyrillic, CJK, etc., reducing download size for pages that primarily use Latin characters.

advanced.question: How do CSS size-adjust and ascent-override/descent-override descriptors affect font rendering?
advanced.answer: These @font-face descriptors adjust font metrics: size-adjust scales the font's glyphs and metrics by a percentage (useful for normalizing different fonts to the same size). ascent-override, descent-override, and line-gap-override override the font's internal metrics to match a target font. These are critical for minimizing layout shift during font swap — you can adjust the fallback font's metrics to match the primary web font, making the swap invisible even with different typefaces.

advanced.question: Explain the interaction between CSS fonts, the OS font fallback system, and the 'emoji' presentation.
advanced.answer: When a character is not supported by any specified font, the OS font fallback system kicks in. For emoji (U+1F300+), the browser typically uses the platform's emoji font (Apple Color Emoji, Segoe UI Emoji, Noto Color Emoji). The font-variant-emoji property controls emoji presentation: text (black-and-white text glyphs) vs emoji (color emoji). The Unicode variation selector U+FE0E (text) and U+FE0F (emoji) also influence presentation. CSS can force a specific presentation with font-family: 'Apple Color Emoji', but this varies by OS.

advanced.question: How does the CSS font matching algorithm work when a specified weight is not available in the font?
advanced.answer: The font matching algorithm: (1) If the exact weight exists, use it. (2) If not, for numeric values < 400, check lower weights first, then higher. (3) For values > 400, check higher weights first, then lower. (4) For value 400, check 500 first, then lower weights. (5) For value 500, check 400 first, then lower, then higher. If no weight exists, the browser performs synthetic bolding (faux bold) — artificially thickening the glyphs, which usually looks inferior to a real bold font.

advanced.question: How do you implement a font loading strategy that prevents both FOIT and excessive CLS?
advanced.answer: A robust strategy: (1) Use font-display: optional for body text (font loads only if fast, otherwise use fallback). (2) Preload the primary font: <link rel="preload" href="font.woff2" as="font" crossorigin>. (3) Use the CSS Font Loading API to detect font load and add a class when ready. (4) Use size-adjust on the @font-face to match fallback metrics to the web font, minimizing layout shift during swap. (5) Consider using a subset font for first paint and the full font for subsequent interaction. (6) Use fallback fonts with similar metrics (Panose-1 matching or manual size-adjust).

advanced.question: How does the CSS spec handle synthetic bold and italic (faux styling) when the font lacks those variants?
advanced.answer: The CSS specification allows browsers to synthesize bold and italic when the font lacks those variants (font-synthesis property controls this). font-synthesis: none; prevents synthetic styling — the text remains normal even if font-weight: bold is set. font-synthesis: weight; allows synthetic bold only. font-synthesis: style; allows synthetic italic/oblique only. font-synthesis: weight style; allows both. Disabling synthesis is recommended when using variable fonts or icon fonts where faux styling would distort glyphs.

advanced.question: Explain the concept of color fonts (COLR, SVG, CBDT/CBLC) and how they're used in CSS.
advanced.answer: Color fonts embed color information within the font file, enabling multi-color glyphs without SVG or images. Formats include: COLR/CPAL (Microsoft/Google, most widely supported), SVG (OpenType SVG, Adobe/Mozilla), and CBDT/CBLC (Google/Emoji). CSS supports color fonts via @font-face with regular font-family — the browser automatically renders color glyphs if the font contains them. The font-palette property (CSS Fonts Level 5) lets you select or define color palettes within color fonts. Color fonts are primarily used for emoji, icons, and decorative lettering.
